summary.brief: Flowers v. Giep MD. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Appellants brought this medical malpractice action against Respondents, alleging Doctor breached the standard of care during Treva's delivery on October 8, 2008.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During delivery, a complication known as shoulder dystocia occurred.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Appellants alleged that Doctor failed to properly manage the shoulder dystocia, which caused Ashley to suffer an injury to her brachial plexus nerves.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court held the statute describes 'two separate and distinct situations' in which a physician cannot be liable for simple negligence and denied Appellants' motion to strike the affirmative defense.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: After deliberating, the jury returned a verdict in favor of Respondents.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury found that although Respondents negligently harmed Ashley, the negligence occurred while Doctor rendered care in a genuine emergency situation.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We find subsection (A) describes a physician that encounters an emergency while providing care whereas subsection (B) describes a physician treating a patient previously unassociated with the physician.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: To adopt Appellants' interpretation and read subsection (B) as a limitation to subsection (A) would be a 'forced construction' of the text's plain language.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Thus, we hold the trial court did not err in its interpretation of section 15-32-230 and in denying Appellants' motion to strike the defense.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: During delivery, a complication known as shoulder dystocia occurred.

reveal.clinical_significance: Requires immediate management to prevent injury
reveal.decision_impact: Prompt management needed to prevent brachial plexus injury

timeline_phases.description: Complication during delivery with shoulder dystocia

decision_points.clinical_context: Management of shoulder dystocia during delivery
decision_points.prompt: How should the shoulder dystocia be managed?

options.description: Perform McRoberts maneuver and apply suprapubic pressure
Attempt to deliver without specific maneuvers
options.clinical_reasoning: Failed to properly manage shoulder dystocia

expected_action_court.description: Perform McRoberts maneuver and apply suprapubic pressure
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Standard obstetric practice for shoulder dystocia

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to perform standard maneuvers for shoulder dystocia
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Standard maneuvers should have been performed

patient_outcome.description: Ashley suffered an injury to her brachial plexus nerves

key_findings: Jury found negligence occurred during a genuine emergency situation
Doctor was not liable due to emergency medical care defense
